title: Data Engineering
desc: Build robust data pipelines, warehouses, and real-time streaming systems

sections.title: The Pipeline Nobody Trusts
sections.content:
Your company ingests data from 20 sources: APIs, databases, CSVs, event streams. Pipelines run as cron jobs. Problems:

• Pipeline 7 failed silently 3 days ago. Nobody noticed.
• The dashboard shows $2.3M revenue. Finance says $2.1M. Nobody knows which is right.
• Adding a new data source takes 3 weeks of custom code.
• When a pipeline fails at step 9 of 12, someone manually re-runs from step 7.

Data engineering is building RELIABLE, OBSERVABLE, SCALABLE systems to move and transform data.

sections.title: See It: ETL vs ELT
sections.content:
ETL (Extract-Transform-Load): Traditional approach.
Extract from source → Transform in a processing engine → Load into warehouse.
Transform happens BEFORE loading. You must know the schema upfront.

ELT (Extract-Load-Transform): Modern approach.
Extract from source → Load RAW into lake/warehouse → Transform using SQL/Spark.
Load everything first, transform later. Schema-on-read. More flexible.

ELT won because: cloud storage is cheap, compute is elastic, and business requirements change faster than schemas.

sections.title: Batch vs Streaming vs Micro-Batch
sections.content:
BATCH: Process data at scheduled intervals.
• Run every hour/day. Process all new data since last run.
• Simple, reliable, well-understood. Tools: Airflow + Spark, dbt.
• Latency: minutes to hours. Acceptable for most analytics.

STREAMING: Process data as it arrives.
• Sub-second latency. Real-time dashboards, fraud detection.
• Complex: ordering, exactly-once semantics, state management.
• Tools: Kafka + Flink, Spark Structured Streaming.

MICRO-BATCH: Batch at very short intervals (seconds).
• Spark Structured Streaming default: micro-batch every few seconds.
• Simpler than true streaming, nearly as fast.
• Good middle ground for 'near-real-time' needs.

Decision framework:
• Dashboard refreshes hourly? → Batch.
• Fraud detection within 1 second? → Streaming.
• 'Real-time' dashboard (30-second delay OK)? → Micro-batch.

80% of data pipelines should be batch. Don't add streaming complexity unless latency requirements demand it.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: • Batch = cron job (scheduled, processes accumulated work) • Micro-batch = setInterval at short intervals • Streaming = event listener (reacts immediately to each event)

sections.title: Idempotency: The Most Important Property
sections.content:
An idempotent pipeline produces the SAME result whether you run it once or ten times.

Why this matters: Pipelines WILL fail. Networks drop. Machines crash. You WILL re-run pipelines. If re-running creates duplicates or corrupts data, you have a problem.

Making pipelines idempotent:

1. UPSERT instead of INSERT:
   MERGE INTO target USING source ON key = key
   WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE
   WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT
   Re-running updates existing records instead of creating duplicates.

2. OVERWRITE partitions instead of appending:
   INSERT OVERWRITE TABLE sales PARTITION (date='2024-01-15')
   Re-running replaces the entire partition.

3. DEDUPLICATION with watermarks:
   Track which data has been processed. On re-run, skip already-processed records.

4. DETERMINISTIC processing:
   Same input → same output. No random IDs, no wall-clock timestamps in logic.

Test: If you run your pipeline 3 times in a row, do you get 3x the data? If yes, it's not idempotent.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: Idempotent pipelines are like HTTP PUT vs POST. • PUT /resource/42 {data} — run it 10 times, resource 42 has the same data. • POST /resources {data} — run it 10 times, you have 10 copies. Always design pipelines as PUT, not POST.

sections.q: Your daily pipeline appends new rows to a fact table. It fails halfway through January 15th's data. You re-run it. Now January 15th has duplicate rows. What's the root cause and fix?
opts: The pipeline uses INSERT (append) instead of MERGE or OVERWRITE — it's not idempotent. Fix: use INSERT OVERWRITE PARTITION (date) or MERGE with a dedup key
sections.explanation: INSERT appends without checking for existing data. Re-running appends again → duplicates. Fix: (1) INSERT OVERWRITE PARTITION replaces the entire day's partition on re-run. (2) MERGE with a primary key updates existing rows and inserts only new ones. (3) Add a dedup step: DELETE FROM table WHERE date = '2024-01-15' before INSERT. All three make the pipeline idempotent — safe to re-run.

sections.title: The Query That Takes 45 Minutes
sections.content:
Analyst asks: 'What's the monthly revenue by product category for the last 2 years?'

Your normalized OLTP schema: orders JOIN order_items JOIN products JOIN categories. The query scans 500M rows across 4 tables. It takes 45 minutes.

The same query on a properly modeled OLAP schema (star schema): one scan of a pre-joined fact table with dimension lookups. 8 seconds.

Data modeling isn't about normal forms. It's about designing schemas for how data is QUERIED.

sections.title: See It: Star Schema
sections.content:
A star schema has two types of tables:

FACT TABLE (center): Events that happened. Each row = one transaction.
fact_orders: order_id, date_key, product_key, customer_key, amount, quantity
Millions to billions of rows. Numeric measures.

DIMENSION TABLES (points of the star): Descriptive attributes.
dim_date: date_key, date, month, quarter, year, is_weekend
dim_product: product_key, name, category, brand, price
dim_customer: customer_key, name, segment, region
Thousands to millions of rows. Text attributes.

Queries JOIN fact to dimensions:
SELECT d.month, p.category, SUM(f.amount)
FROM fact_orders f JOIN dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key
JOIN dim_product p ON f.product_key = p.product_key
GROUP BY d.month, p.category

sections.title: Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD)
sections.content:
A customer moves from New York to London. What happens to historical reports?

SCD TYPE 1 — Overwrite:
UPDATE dim_customer SET city = 'London' WHERE id = 42
Past reports now show 'London' for all historical orders. History is lost.
Use when: You don't care about historical accuracy (e.g., fixing a typo).

SCD TYPE 2 — Add new row:
INSERT: id=42, city='London', valid_from='2024-06-01', valid_to='9999-12-31', is_current=true
UPDATE old row: valid_to='2024-05-31', is_current=false
Historical reports show 'New York' for old orders, 'London' for new ones.
Use when: Historical accuracy matters (most cases).

SCD TYPE 3 — Add new column:
city_current = 'London', city_previous = 'New York'
Only tracks one change. Rarely used.

SCD Type 2 is the most common and most important to understand. The valid_from/valid_to pattern enables point-in-time queries:
SELECT * FROM dim_customer
WHERE customer_id = 42 AND '2024-03-15' BETWEEN valid_from AND valid_to
→ Returns the New York row (correct for March 2024).
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: SCD types mirror version control strategies: • Type 1 = git push --force (overwrite history) • Type 2 = git commit (preserve full history, latest is HEAD) • Type 3 = git stash (save one previous state)

sections.title: OLTP vs OLAP: Two Different Worlds
sections.content:
OLTP (Online Transaction Processing):
Purpose: Run the business. Process transactions.
Queries: Point lookups. SELECT * FROM orders WHERE id = 12345
Schema: Normalized (3NF). Minimize redundancy.
Rows: Millions. Each is small.
Optimized for: Write speed, consistency, row-level operations.
Examples: PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle.

OLAP (Online Analytical Processing):
Purpose: Analyze the business. Generate insights.
Queries: Aggregations. SELECT category, SUM(revenue) GROUP BY category
Schema: Denormalized (star/snowflake). Pre-joined for query speed.
Rows: Billions. Columnar storage.
Optimized for: Read speed, scan throughput, column-level operations.
Examples: BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift, Databricks SQL.

Columnar vs Row storage:
Row store: [id=1, name='Alice', age=30] [id=2, name='Bob', age=25] ...
Column store: ids=[1,2,...] names=['Alice','Bob',...] ages=[30,25,...]

Query: SELECT AVG(age) FROM users
Row store: Must read every column of every row. 90% of data read is wasted.
Column store: Only reads the 'age' column. 10x less I/O. Plus: same-type data compresses better (ages: 30,25,31,28 → delta encode).
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: Row vs Column storage is like array-of-structs vs struct-of-arrays. • Row = [{name,age,email}, {name,age,email}, ...] — good for accessing one complete record • Column = {names: [...], ages: [...], emails: [...]} — good for scanning one field across all records

sections.q: Your fact_orders table has 2 billion rows. A query filtering WHERE product_category = 'Electronics' scans all 2B rows because product_category is in dim_product, not in fact_orders. How do you optimize?
opts: Add product_category as a column in fact_orders (denormalize) so the filter can be applied directly without joining dim_product first
sections.explanation: In OLAP, denormalization is the norm. Adding product_category directly to fact_orders means the query engine can filter 2B rows down to 200M (Electronics only) BEFORE joining dim_product for other attributes. This is a 10x reduction in join size. The tradeoff: storage increases slightly and you need to keep the denormalized column in sync. But storage is cheap and the query speedup is dramatic. This is why star schemas prioritize query performance over normalization.

sections.title: When Batch Is Too Slow
sections.content:
Your ride-sharing app updates surge pricing hourly via batch pipeline. But demand changes by the minute. At 5 PM, a concert ends and 50,000 people request rides simultaneously. Your hourly batch won't update prices for another 40 minutes. Riders see stale prices. Drivers don't know about the surge.

You need STREAMING: process events as they happen, not in batches.

sections.title: See It: Kafka Architecture
sections.content:
Kafka is a distributed event log:

PRODUCERS write events to TOPICS.
Topics are split into PARTITIONS (for parallelism).
CONSUMERS read from partitions.

Key insight: Kafka is an append-only LOG, not a queue. Messages are NOT deleted after consumption. Multiple consumers can read the same data independently.

Retention: Keep messages for 7 days (or forever). New consumers can replay from any point in history.

sections.title: Kafka Core Concepts
sections.content:
TOPICS: Named channels for events.
topic: ride-requests, topic: driver-locations, topic: surge-updates

PARTITIONS: Topics are split for parallelism.
ride-requests has 12 partitions. 12 consumers can read in parallel.
Messages within a partition are ORDERED. Across partitions: no order guarantee.

KEYS: Determine which partition a message goes to.
key=user_id ensures all events for one user go to the same partition → ordered per user.

CONSUMER GROUPS: Multiple consumers sharing the load.
3 consumers in group 'pricing-service' split 12 partitions: each reads 4.
A different group 'analytics-service' reads ALL 12 partitions independently.

OFFSETS: Position in the partition log.
Consumer tracks: 'I've processed up to offset 42,387 in partition 5.'
Crash and restart → resume from offset 42,388. No reprocessing.

DELIVERY SEMANTICS:
• At-most-once: Commit offset before processing. Fast but may lose data.
• At-least-once: Process, then commit offset. Safe but may get duplicates. (Default)
• Exactly-once: Kafka transactions. Complex but no loss and no duplicates.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: Kafka is like a NEWSPAPER printing press: • Topics = newspaper sections (Sports, Business, Tech) • Partitions = printing presses running in parallel • Producers = journalists filing stories • Consumers = subscribers reading sections • Key insight: the press keeps a permanent archive. New subscribers can read ALL past issues.

sections.title: Stream Processing Patterns
sections.content:
FILTERING: Keep only relevant events.
ride-requests → filter(city='NYC') → nyc-ride-requests

TRANSFORMATION: Enrich or reshape events.
ride-request + driver-location → ride-with-eta

AGGREGATION: Compute over time windows.
'Average surge multiplier in the last 5 minutes per zone.'
Window types:
• TUMBLING: Fixed, non-overlapping. [0:00-0:05], [0:05-0:10]
• SLIDING: Fixed, overlapping. Every event triggers a window of last 5 min.
• SESSION: Dynamic, gap-based. Window closes after 30 min of inactivity.

JOIN: Combine two streams.
ride-requests JOIN driver-locations ON zone within 5-minute window.

LATE ARRIVALS: A ride request from 2 minutes ago arrives now (network delay). How do you handle it?
WATERMARK: 'I expect all events within a 3-minute delay. After that, drop or send to a late-arrivals topic.'
Trade-off: Longer watermark → more complete but higher latency.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: Stream processing windows are like database GROUP BY with a time dimension: • Tumbling = GROUP BY FLOOR(timestamp / interval) • Sliding = GROUP BY each timestamp with a RANGE window • Session = GROUP BY user with a timeout gap

sections.q: Your Kafka topic has 6 partitions. You deploy 8 consumers in the same consumer group. What happens?
opts: 6 consumers each get 1 partition. 2 consumers sit IDLE — in a consumer group, each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer. More consumers than partitions means wasted resources
sections.explanation: Kafka's consumer group protocol assigns each partition to exactly one consumer. With 6 partitions and 8 consumers, 6 consumers each get 1 partition and 2 are idle (no partitions to read). To scale beyond 6 consumers, you need more partitions. Rule: max parallelism = number of partitions. Plan partition count for peak consumer needs. Note: you CAN have fewer consumers than partitions (each consumer handles multiple partitions).

sections.title: The Cron Job Graveyard
sections.content:
Your data team has 47 cron jobs running on 3 different machines. Some depend on each other but there's no dependency management. Cron job 12 runs at 3 AM but depends on cron job 8 which runs at 2:30 AM — usually finishes by 3 AM, but on Mondays it takes 90 minutes.

Monday mornings: job 12 processes stale data. Dashboard is wrong until someone notices and manually reruns.

You need an ORCHESTRATOR: define dependencies, handle failures, retry automatically, and provide visibility into what's running.

sections.title: See It: Airflow DAG
sections.content:
Airflow uses DAGs (Directed Acyclic Graphs) to define pipelines:

extract_orders → clean_orders → load_orders_silver → ↘
extract_users → clean_users → load_users_silver → → build_gold_revenue
extract_products → clean_products → load_products_silver → ↗

Each node is a TASK. Arrows are DEPENDENCIES.
Airflow runs tasks in dependency order. If extract_orders fails, nothing downstream runs.
Built-in: retries, alerts, backfill, monitoring UI.

sections.title: Airflow Core Concepts
sections.content:
DAG: A Python file defining the pipeline structure.
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator

with DAG('daily_revenue', schedule='@daily', start_date=datetime(2024,1,1), catchup=False):
  extract = PythonOperator(task_id='extract', python_callable=extract_fn)
  transform = PythonOperator(task_id='transform', python_callable=transform_fn)
  load = PythonOperator(task_id='load', python_callable=load_fn)
  extract >> transform >> load  # dependency chain

OPERATORS: Types of tasks.
• PythonOperator: Run a Python function
• BashOperator: Run a shell command
• SparkSubmitOperator: Submit a Spark job
• SqlOperator: Execute SQL

SCHEDULE: When the DAG runs.
@daily, @hourly, '0 3 * * *' (cron), or triggered externally.

BACKFILL: Re-run historical dates.
airflow dags backfill --start-date 2024-01-01 --end-date 2024-01-31
Re-processes all of January. Each run gets its execution_date for idempotency.

XCOM: Pass small data between tasks.
extract pushes output path → transform pulls it.
NOT for large datasets. Use files/tables for data, XCom for metadata.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: Airflow is a CI/CD system for data: • DAG = pipeline YAML file • Tasks = pipeline stages • Schedule = trigger (cron, on push) • Backfill = re-running historical builds • XCom = pipeline artifacts/outputs

sections.title: Modern Orchestration Alternatives
sections.content:
AIRFLOW: The established standard. 10+ years. Massive ecosystem.
Pros: Battle-tested, huge community, tons of operators.
Cons: Complex setup, DAGs as Python code can be messy, scheduler bottleneck.

DAGSTER: Software-defined data assets.
Define WHAT (assets), not HOW (tasks). Focuses on data lineage.
Pros: Better testing, type system, development experience.
Cons: Smaller ecosystem, newer.

PREFECT: Dynamic, Pythonic workflows.
Pros: No DAG files needed, dynamic task creation, cloud-native.
Cons: Less mature ecosystem.

MAGE: Modern, notebook-like interface.
Pros: Developer-friendly, built-in data integration.
Cons: Newest, smallest community.

DATABRICKS WORKFLOWS / dbt Cloud:
Managed orchestration within their ecosystems.
Pros: Zero infrastructure. Tight integration.
Cons: Vendor lock-in.

Choosing:
• Enterprise with existing Airflow → stay with Airflow.
• Greenfield project → consider Dagster or Prefect.
• Databricks-heavy → Databricks Workflows.
• SQL-heavy transforms → dbt Cloud.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: Orchestrator evolution mirrors CI/CD evolution: • Airflow = Jenkins (powerful, configurable, complex) • Dagster = GitHub Actions (declarative, integrated) • Prefect = CircleCI (cloud-native, simple) • dbt Cloud = Vercel (specialized, managed)

sections.q: Your Airflow DAG runs daily at 3 AM. The extract task takes 10 minutes usually but occasionally takes 2 hours (API rate limiting). The transform task starts at 3:10 AM regardless. What breaks?
opts: If extract is still running at 3:10, Airflow does NOT start transform because transform depends on extract — Airflow respects dependencies. But the NEXT day's DAG run might start at 3 AM while yesterday's is still running. Fix: set max_active_runs=1 and appropriate timeouts
sections.explanation: Airflow respects task dependencies — transform waits for extract. But without max_active_runs=1, the next day's DAG run can start while yesterday's is still in progress. Two runs of the same pipeline running simultaneously can cause race conditions (writing to the same table). Also set execution_timeout on tasks to catch runaway jobs, and configure SLA alerts to notify when extract takes longer than expected.

sections.title: 1,200 Lines of SQL Nobody Understands
sections.content:
Your Gold layer is built by one massive SQL query: 1,200 lines, 14 CTEs, 8 JOINs, nested subqueries. It was written by someone who left. It takes 45 minutes to run. When it breaks, nobody can debug it.

Worse: three other queries copy-paste the same customer logic (each with slight variations). When the customer definition changes, you update one but miss the others.

dbt (data build tool) solves this: modular SQL models with dependency management, testing, and documentation.

sections.title: See It: dbt Model Dependency Graph
sections.content:
dbt builds a DAG from SQL model files:

stg_orders.sql → int_orders_enriched.sql → ↘
stg_customers.sql → int_customer_metrics.sql → → mart_revenue.sql
stg_products.sql → ↗

Each .sql file is a SELECT statement. dbt handles the CREATE TABLE / INSERT.
Ref other models: SELECT * FROM {{ ref('stg_orders') }}
dbt builds them in dependency order automatically.

sections.title: dbt Project Structure
sections.content:
STAGING MODELS (stg_): 1:1 with source tables. Rename columns, cast types.
-- models/staging/stg_orders.sql
SELECT
  id AS order_id,
  created_at::timestamp AS ordered_at,
  amount::decimal(10,2) AS order_amount,
  user_id AS customer_id
FROM {{ source('raw', 'orders') }}

INTERMEDIATE MODELS (int_): Business logic. Joins, enrichment.
-- models/intermediate/int_orders_enriched.sql
SELECT o.*, c.segment, c.region
FROM {{ ref('stg_orders') }} o
JOIN {{ ref('stg_customers') }} c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id

MART MODELS (mart_): Final tables consumed by BI tools.
-- models/marts/mart_revenue.sql
SELECT date_trunc('month', ordered_at) AS month,
  region, segment, SUM(order_amount) AS revenue
FROM {{ ref('int_orders_enriched') }}
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3

TESTING:
-- tests on stg_orders:
- unique: order_id
- not_null: [order_id, customer_id, order_amount]
- accepted_values: { column: status, values: ['pending','completed','cancelled'] }
- relationships: { to: ref('stg_customers'), field: customer_id }

dbt test runs ALL tests. Catch data quality issues before they reach dashboards.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: dbt is a BUILD SYSTEM for SQL: • Models = source files • ref() = import statements • dbt run = compile + execute • dbt test = unit tests • Lineage graph = module dependency tree • Staging/intermediate/marts = src/lib/dist

sections.title: Materialization Strategies
sections.content:
How dbt creates the output table:

VIEW (default): CREATE VIEW. No data stored. Computed on query.
Pros: Always fresh. No storage cost.
Cons: Slow for complex logic (recomputed every time).
Use for: Staging models, simple transforms.

TABLE: CREATE TABLE AS SELECT. Data materialized.
Pros: Fast reads (pre-computed).
Cons: Stale between runs. Full rebuild every time.
Use for: Mart models queried frequently.

INCREMENTAL: Only process NEW data since last run.
{{ config(materialized='incremental', unique_key='order_id') }}
SELECT * FROM {{ ref('stg_orders') }}
{% if is_incremental() %}
WHERE ordered_at > (SELECT MAX(ordered_at) FROM {{ this }})
{% endif %}

First run: Full table. Subsequent runs: Only new rows.
Pros: Fast for large tables (process 1 day, not 3 years).
Cons: Complex. Must handle late arrivals, schema changes.
Use for: Large fact tables with append-mostly data.

EPHEMERAL: Not materialized at all. Inlined as CTE in downstream models.
Use for: Simple helper transforms used in one place.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: Materializations mirror caching strategies: • View = no cache (compute on every request) • Table = full cache rebuild (invalidate + recompute entirely) • Incremental = cache with delta updates (only refresh what changed) • Ephemeral = inline code (no separate artifact)

sections.q: Your incremental model processes orders by ordered_at. An order from 3 days ago arrives late (source system delay). Your incremental model only looks at rows WHERE ordered_at > max(ordered_at). The late order is missed. Fix?
opts: Use a lookback window: WHERE ordered_at > max(ordered_at) - INTERVAL '3 days'. Re-process the last 3 days on every run to catch late arrivals, with MERGE to prevent duplicates
sections.explanation: Late-arriving data is the most common incremental model bug. Fix: add a lookback window that re-processes the last N days (where N = your maximum expected delay). Combined with unique_key in the incremental config, dbt uses MERGE to upsert — existing rows are updated, new rows are inserted, no duplicates. The cost: re-processing 3 days of data instead of 1 day. For most tables, this is a small price for correctness.

sections.title: The $2M Dashboard Mistake
sections.content:
The revenue dashboard shows $4.7M for Q4. The CEO presents this to the board. Finance finds the real number is $2.5M — a pipeline silently double-counted transactions from a source system migration.

Nobody caught it because there were no data quality checks. No anomaly detection. No reconciliation against the source. The dashboard was trusted because it existed, not because it was verified.

sections.title: See It: Data Quality Dimensions
sections.content:
Six dimensions of data quality:

COMPLETENESS: Are all expected records present? (No missing days)
UNIQUENESS: No duplicate records? (Primary key is unique)
VALIDITY: Values within expected ranges? (Age between 0-150)
ACCURACY: Values match reality? (Revenue matches finance system)
TIMELINESS: Data is fresh enough? (Dashboard reflects yesterday's data)
CONSISTENCY: Same fact gives same answer everywhere? (Revenue matches across reports)

sections.title: Testing at Every Layer
sections.content:
SCHEMA TESTS (every run):
• Not null: Critical columns always have values
• Unique: Primary keys are truly unique
• Accepted values: Status IN ('active','inactive','pending')
• Relationships: Every order.customer_id exists in customers
Tools: dbt tests, Great Expectations.

VOLUME TESTS (every run):
• Row count within expected range (yesterday ±20%)
• No missing dates in date-partitioned tables
• Source-to-target reconciliation (source count = target count)

ANOMALY DETECTION (daily):
• Revenue today vs 7-day average. Alert if >50% deviation.
• NULL rate spike (0.1% → 15% = something broke upstream)
• Cardinality changes (suddenly 500 new product categories?)

CROSS-SYSTEM RECONCILIATION (weekly):
• Sum(revenue) in warehouse = Sum(revenue) in finance system
• User count in analytics = User count in production DB
• If they differ: investigation, not panic.

FRESHNESS CHECKS (continuous):
• 'Silver customers table was last updated 2 hours ago. Expected: hourly.'
• Alert if data is staler than SLA.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: Data testing mirrors software testing: • Schema tests = type checking (compile-time guarantees) • Volume tests = smoke tests (does it roughly work?) • Anomaly detection = performance monitoring (catch regressions) • Reconciliation = integration tests (components agree) • Freshness = uptime monitoring (system is responsive)

sections.title: Great Expectations & Data Contracts
sections.content:
GREAT EXPECTATIONS: Python framework for data validation.
import great_expectations as gx
validator = gx.read_csv('orders.csv')
validator.expect_column_values_to_not_be_null('order_id')
validator.expect_column_values_to_be_between('amount', 0, 100000)
validator.expect_column_pair_values_to_be_unique(['order_id', 'line_item'])
results = validator.validate()

DATA CONTRACTS: Formal agreements between producers and consumers.

Producer (payments team) guarantees:
• Schema: {payment_id: string, amount: decimal, currency: enum(USD,EUR,GBP)}
• Freshness: Updated within 1 hour
• Volume: 10K-500K records/day
• Quality: amount > 0, no nulls in payment_id

Consumer (analytics team) depends on these guarantees.

If the producer changes the schema or violates quality guarantees → contract violation → alert BEFORE downstream dashboards break.

This shifts quality LEFT — catch issues at the source, not when the CEO sees wrong numbers.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: Data contracts are like API contracts (OpenAPI specs): • Schema = request/response schema • Freshness SLA = latency SLA • Volume = rate limits • Quality = validation rules Both prevent breaking changes from propagating to consumers.

sections.q: Your daily pipeline loads 100K orders/day. One day it loads 250K. No errors in logs. The data looks normal. What happened and how should your quality system catch this?
opts: Likely a duplicate load or source system sent historical backfill. A volume anomaly test (row count vs 7-day average, alert if >2x deviation) would catch this automatically before data reaches dashboards
sections.explanation: 2.5x the normal volume is almost certainly not organic growth in one day. Common causes: (1) Source system sent a backfill of historical data mixed with today's data. (2) Pipeline ran twice (not idempotent). (3) A schema change caused row explosion in a JOIN. A volume test comparing today's count against the rolling average (±50% threshold) would immediately flag this. Combined with a dedup check on primary keys, you'd know whether it's duplicates or genuinely new data. Catch it before it hits dashboards.

sections.title: The $80K Monthly Cloud Bill
sections.content:
Your data team's cloud bill: $80K/month. Breakdown:
• $30K: Spark clusters running 24/7 (utilized 4 hours/day)
• $20K: Snowflake warehouse running queries 8 hours/day
• $15K: Data stored in S3 Standard (70% not accessed in 6 months)
• $15K: Various services, untagged, unknown owners

The CTO asks: 'Can we get this under $40K without losing capability?' The answer is yes.

sections.title: See It: Cost Optimization Layers
sections.content:
Four layers of cost optimization:

COMPUTE: Right-size, auto-terminate, spot instances
STORAGE: Tiering, compression, lifecycle policies
QUERY: Partition pruning, caching, materialization
GOVERNANCE: Tagging, ownership, budget alerts

Typically 40-60% savings achievable without losing any capability.

sections.title: Compute & Storage Optimization
sections.content:
COMPUTE:
• AUTO-TERMINATE clusters after idle timeout (15-30 min)
  $30K/month running 24/7 → $5K running only during jobs
• SPOT/PREEMPTIBLE instances for batch jobs: 60-80% cheaper
• RIGHT-SIZE: Monitor CPU/memory utilization. Downsize over-provisioned clusters.
• SERVERLESS where possible: Pay per query, not per hour.

STORAGE:
• LIFECYCLE POLICIES: Move data not accessed in 30 days to cold storage.
  S3 Standard: $0.023/GB → S3 Glacier: $0.004/GB (6x cheaper)
• COMPRESSION: Parquet + Snappy compression = 80% smaller than CSV
  1 TB CSV → 200 GB Parquet. Storage cost: $23 → $4.60
• PARTITIONING: Queries scan only relevant partitions, reducing compute cost.
  SELECT FROM orders WHERE date = '2024-01-15' scans 1 day, not 3 years.
• VACUUM / COMPACT: Remove old Delta Lake versions. Compact small files.

QUERY OPTIMIZATION:
• MATERIALIZED VIEWS: Pre-compute expensive aggregations.
• RESULT CACHING: Identical queries return cached results.
• QUERY GOVERNANCE: Kill queries running longer than 30 minutes.
  Often a missing WHERE clause scanning the entire table.

The biggest single savings: auto-terminating idle clusters. Most teams leave them running 24/7 but use them <8 hours.
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: Data cost optimization mirrors application cost optimization: • Auto-terminate = serverless functions (pay for execution time only) • Storage tiering = CDN vs origin (frequently accessed = fast storage, archival = cheap storage) • Query optimization = database query optimization (index, partition, cache) • Governance = resource quotas in Kubernetes

sections.title: Building a Cost-Aware Culture
sections.content:
Technical fixes are necessary but not sufficient. You need a CULTURE where cost is visible.

TAG EVERYTHING: Every resource tagged with: team, project, environment.
• Team: 'data-engineering', 'ml-platform', 'analytics'
• Project: 'customer-360', 'fraud-detection'
• Environment: 'prod', 'staging', 'dev'

COST DASHBOARDS: Weekly email/Slack showing each team's spend vs budget.
'ML Platform: $12,400 this week (budget: $15,000). Top cost: GPU training cluster.'

SHOWBACK / CHARGEBACK:
• Showback: 'Your team's Snowflake credits cost $8,200 this month.' (informational)
• Chargeback: That $8,200 comes out of your team's budget. (real consequences)
Chargeback drives behavior change. Showback creates awareness.

FINOPS PRACTICES:
• Monthly cost review with engineering leads
• Anomaly alerts: 'Daily spend jumped 40% vs last week'
• Pre-approval for resources over $X/month
• Quarterly optimization sprints: review and right-size everything
sections.analogy: Tech analogy: FinOps is like performance engineering for money: • Cost dashboards = performance monitoring • Tagging = distributed tracing (attribute cost to source) • Budget alerts = latency alerts • Right-sizing = performance optimization • Chargeback = SLOs (accountability for resource consumption)

sections.q: Your Snowflake warehouse runs 24/7 at 'Large' size but is only queried 9 AM-5 PM on weekdays. What's the simplest optimization?
opts: Enable auto-suspend (pause after 5 min idle) and auto-resume (restart on query). This alone cuts costs by ~75% since the warehouse runs 40 hours/week instead of 168
sections.explanation: Auto-suspend + auto-resume is the single biggest cost lever for Snowflake (and similar). Active 40 hours/week instead of 168 = 76% cost reduction. The warehouse pauses within minutes of idle and restarts in seconds when a query arrives. Users experience a 1-2 second cold start on the first query after resume — barely noticeable. Combine with right-sizing (if average CPU is <30%, downsize) and multi-cluster scaling (auto-scale on concurrency rather than keeping one big warehouse) for additional savings.
